sectionTitle: 4.9 Periodic Trends: Ionization Energy and Electron Affinity
expanded_description: Energy trends in the periodic table

core_concept.principle: Ionization energy and electron affinity are fundamental periodic properties that determine how easily atoms lose or gain electrons, directly influencing chemical reactivity and bonding behavior.
core_concept.explanation: Ionization energy measures the energy required to remove an electron from an atom, while electron affinity measures the energy change when an atom gains an electron. Both properties follow predictable trends across the periodic table based on atomic radius, nuclear charge, and electron shielding.

image.alt: Periodic table showing ionization energy and electron affinity trends with arrows
image.caption: Ionization energy increases across periods and decreases down groups; electron affinity generally becomes more negative across periods.

sections.introduction: Ionization energy is a fundamental atomic property that measures how strongly an atom holds onto its electrons, directly affecting chemical reactivity and bonding patterns.

introduction_image.alt: Diagram showing electron being removed from atom with energy input
introduction_image.caption: Ionization energy is the minimum energy required to remove an electron from a gaseous atom.

key_concept.definition: Ionization energy (IE) is the minimum energy required to remove an electron from a gaseous atom or ion in its ground state, measured in kilojoules per mole (kJ/mol) or electron volts (eV).
key_concept.context: First ionization energy removes the outermost electron: $M(g) \rightarrow M^+(g) + e^-$. Successive ionization energies remove additional electrons, each requiring more energy than the previous.

image.alt: Equation showing first ionization energy process
image.caption: First ionization energy removes the most loosely held valence electron.

sections.section_title: 4.9.2 Periodic Trend: Ionization Energy Across Periods
sections.introduction: Moving left to right across a period, ionization energy generally increases due to increasing effective nuclear charge with electrons in the same principal energy level.

classifications.value: Increasing proton number strengthens the attraction between nucleus and valence electrons
characteristics: Nuclear charge ($Z$) increases by 1 for each element
Electrons are added to the same principal energy level ($n$)
Shielding by inner electrons remains relatively constant
Effective nuclear charge ($Z_{eff}$) increases across the period
classifications.behavior: As $Z_{eff}$ increases, valence electrons are held more tightly, requiring more energy to remove them.

examples.explanation: Na ($496$ kJ/mol) < Mg ($738$ kJ/mol) < Al ($578$ kJ/mol) < Si ($787$ kJ/mol) < P ($1012$ kJ/mol) < S ($1000$ kJ/mol) < Cl ($1251$ kJ/mol) < Ar ($1521$ kJ/mol). General increase with minor exceptions.

image.alt: Graph showing ionization energy increasing across Period 3
image.caption: Ionization energy generally increases across periods with some exceptions.

classifications.value: Small decreases occur between Groups 2-13 and 15-16 due to electron configuration effects
characteristics: Group 2 to 13: $ns^2$ to $ns^2 np^1$ - p electron is easier to remove than s electron
Group 15 to 16: $ns^2 np^3$ to $ns^2 np^4$ - electron-electron repulsion in paired p orbital
Half-filled and fully-filled subshells have extra stability
These exceptions confirm the importance of electron configuration
classifications.behavior: Electron configuration effects can override the general $Z_{eff}$ trend, creating local decreases in ionization energy.

examples.explanation: Be ($1s^2 2s^2$) has IE = $899$ kJ/mol; B ($1s^2 2s^2 2p^1$) has IE = $801$ kJ/mol. The 2p electron in B is higher energy and easier to remove than 2s electron in Be.

image.alt: Orbital diagrams showing Be and B electron configurations
image.caption: s electrons are held more tightly than p electrons in the same energy level.

examples.explanation: N ($2p^3$, half-filled) has IE = $1402$ kJ/mol; O ($2p^4$, one paired electron) has IE = $1314$ kJ/mol. Electron-electron repulsion in O's paired 2p orbital makes removal easier.

image.alt: Orbital diagrams showing N and O electron configurations
image.caption: Half-filled subshells have extra stability, increasing ionization energy.

sections.section_title: 4.9.3 Periodic Trend: Ionization Energy Down Groups
sections.introduction: Moving down a group, ionization energy decreases systematically due to increasing atomic size and electron shielding.

principles.statement: Valence electrons in higher principal energy levels are farther from the nucleus and experience weaker attraction.
principles.application: Group 1 ionization energies: Li ($520$ kJ/mol) > Na ($496$ kJ/mol) > K ($419$ kJ/mol) > Rb ($403$ kJ/mol) > Cs ($376$ kJ/mol). Each step adds a new energy level.
principles.analogy: Like a magnet and paperclip: the farther the paperclip (electron) is from the magnet (nucleus), the easier it is to pull away.
principles.example: Cesium's valence electron is in $n=6$, much farther from the nucleus than lithium's $n=2$ electron, making Cs much easier to ionize.

image.alt: Graph showing ionization energy decreasing down Group 1
image.caption: Ionization energy decreases down groups as valence electrons are farther from nucleus.

principles.statement: Inner electron shells shield valence electrons from the full nuclear charge, reducing effective attraction.
principles.application: As more inner shells are added down a group, shielding increases. Despite increasing nuclear charge, $Z_{eff}$ experienced by valence electrons remains similar or decreases slightly.
principles.analogy: Like layers of clothing: more layers (inner shells) between you and the heat source (nucleus) means you feel less heat (attraction).
principles.example: Potassium has 18 inner electrons shielding its $4s^1$ valence electron, making it easier to remove than sodium's $3s^1$ electron with only 10 inner electrons.

image.alt: Diagram showing electron shielding in larger atoms
image.caption: Inner electrons shield valence electrons, reducing ionization energy down groups.

sections.introduction: Removing multiple electrons from an atom requires progressively more energy, with dramatic jumps when removing core electrons.

worked_examples.title: Analyzing Successive Ionization Energies
worked_examples.problem: The successive ionization energies for an element are: IE₁ = $578$ kJ/mol, IE₂ = $1817$ kJ/mol, IE₃ = $2745$ kJ/mol, IE₄ = $11578$ kJ/mol, IE₅ = $14831$ kJ/mol. Identify the element's group and explain the pattern.

step_by_step.explanation: Examine the pattern of ionization energies. Look for large jumps that indicate removal of core electrons.
step_by_step.calculation: IE₁ to IE₂: $1817/578 = 3.1×$ increase; IE₂ to IE₃: $2745/1817 = 1.5×$ increase; IE₃ to IE₄: $11578/2745 = 4.2×$ increase

step_by_step.explanation: The large jump between IE₃ and IE₄ indicates that the first 3 electrons are valence electrons, and the 4th electron is from a core shell.
step_by_step.calculation: Large jump after IE₃ indicates 3 valence electrons

step_by_step.explanation: Elements with 3 valence electrons are in Group 13. The IE values match aluminum (Al).
Verify: Removing 3 valence electrons ($3s^2 3p^1$) gives Al³⁺ with noble gas configuration. Removing 4th electron breaks into stable $[Ne]$ core.

worked_examples.final_answer: The element is aluminum (Group 13). The large jump between IE₃ and IE₄ indicates 3 valence electrons, with the 4th electron coming from a stable noble gas core.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Successive ionization energies reveal the number of valence electrons: large jumps occur when removing core electrons after all valence electrons are removed.

image.alt: Graph showing successive ionization energies with large jump after valence electrons
image.caption: Large jumps in successive IE indicate removal of core electrons.

sections.introduction: Electron affinity measures the energy change when an atom gains an electron, indicating how readily an atom forms negative ions.

categories.description: Electron affinity (EA) is the energy change when an electron is added to a gaseous atom: $X(g) + e^- \rightarrow X^-(g)$. Most values are negative (energy released).
categories.detailed_mechanism: Negative EA means energy is released when the electron is added (favorable process). Positive EA means energy must be added (unfavorable). More negative EA indicates greater tendency to gain electrons.

examples.explanation: Cl has EA = $-349$ kJ/mol (very negative). Adding an electron completes the $3p$ subshell, releasing substantial energy. Chlorine readily forms Cl⁻ ions.

image.alt: Chlorine atom gaining electron to form chloride ion
image.caption: Halogens have very negative electron affinities, readily forming anions.

examples.explanation: Ne has EA > 0 (positive). Adding an electron would require placing it in a higher energy level ($3s$), which is unfavorable. Noble gases do not form stable anions.

image.alt: Noble gas with full valence shell not accepting electron
image.caption: Noble gases have positive electron affinities, resisting anion formation.

categories.description: Electron affinity generally becomes more negative (more favorable) across a period from left to right, with exceptions.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Increasing $Z_{eff}$ across a period makes atoms more attractive to additional electrons. Halogens (Group 17) have the most negative EA values. Group 18 (noble gases) and Group 2 (alkaline earth metals) have positive or slightly negative EA.

examples.explanation: Li ($-60$ kJ/mol) < Be (>0) < B ($-27$ kJ/mol) < C ($-122$ kJ/mol) < N (>0) < O ($-141$ kJ/mol) < F ($-328$ kJ/mol) < Ne (>0). General trend toward more negative with exceptions.

image.alt: Graph showing electron affinity trend across Period 2
image.caption: Electron affinity becomes more negative across periods with exceptions.

categories.description: Electron affinity generally becomes less negative (less favorable) down a group, but trends are less regular than ionization energy.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Larger atoms have valence electrons farther from nucleus, reducing attraction for additional electrons. However, electron-electron repulsion in smaller atoms can make EA less negative than expected (e.g., F vs. Cl).

examples.explanation: F ($-328$ kJ/mol) < Cl ($-349$ kJ/mol) > Br ($-325$ kJ/mol) > I ($-295$ kJ/mol). Chlorine has more negative EA than fluorine due to less electron-electron repulsion in larger Cl atom.

image.alt: Graph showing electron affinity trend down Group 17
image.caption: Electron affinity trend down groups is less regular than ionization energy.

sections.section_title: 4.9.6 Comparing Ionization Energy and Electron Affinity
sections.introduction: Ionization energy and electron affinity are complementary properties that together determine an element's tendency to form cations or anions.

process_flow.description: Elements with low ionization energy and moderately negative electron affinity tend to form cations.
process_flow.mechanism: Easy to lose electrons (low IE), but gaining electrons is not highly favorable. Metals typically show this pattern.
process_flow.result: Forms positive ions (cations) in ionic compounds.

image.alt: Metal atom losing electron to form cation
image.caption: Low IE favors cation formation in metals.

process_flow.description: Elements with high ionization energy and very negative electron affinity tend to form anions.
process_flow.mechanism: Difficult to lose electrons (high IE), but gaining electrons is highly favorable. Nonmetals typically show this pattern.
process_flow.result: Forms negative ions (anions) in ionic compounds.

image.alt: Nonmetal atom gaining electron to form anion
image.caption: Very negative EA favors anion formation in nonmetals.

process_flow.description: Elements with high ionization energy and positive or slightly negative electron affinity tend to share electrons (covalent bonding).
process_flow.mechanism: Neither losing nor gaining electrons is favorable. These elements typically form covalent bonds by sharing electrons.
process_flow.result: Forms covalent compounds through electron sharing.

image.alt: Two atoms sharing electrons in covalent bond
image.caption: High IE and low EA favor covalent bonding through electron sharing.

sections.section_title: 4.9.7 Factors Affecting Ionization Energy and Electron Affinity
sections.introduction: Multiple atomic properties influence ionization energy and electron affinity, and understanding their interplay helps explain periodic trends and exceptions.

principles.statement: Larger atomic radius means valence electrons are farther from the nucleus, reducing attraction and lowering ionization energy.
principles.application: Down a group: radius increases, IE decreases. Across a period: radius decreases, IE increases. This is the dominant factor for group trends.
principles.analogy: Like gravity and distance: the farther you are from Earth (nucleus), the less gravitational pull (attraction) you feel, making it easier to escape (ionize).
principles.example: Cs (radius $265$ pm) has IE = $376$ kJ/mol; Li (radius $152$ pm) has IE = $520$ kJ/mol. Larger Cs atom has lower IE.

image.alt: Diagram showing relationship between atomic radius and ionization energy
image.caption: Larger atomic radius correlates with lower ionization energy.

principles.statement: Higher nuclear charge increases attraction for electrons, raising ionization energy and making electron affinity more negative.
principles.application: Across a period: $Z$ increases, IE increases, EA becomes more negative. This is the dominant factor for period trends.
principles.analogy: Like a stronger magnet: more protons (stronger magnet) hold electrons (metal objects) more tightly, requiring more energy to remove them.
principles.example: F ($Z=9$) has IE = $1681$ kJ/mol; O ($Z=8$) has IE = $1314$ kJ/mol. Higher $Z$ in F means higher IE.

image.alt: Diagram showing nuclear charge effect on electron attraction
image.caption: Higher nuclear charge increases electron attraction, raising IE and making EA more negative.

principles.statement: Stable electron configurations (full or half-full subshells) increase ionization energy and can make electron affinity less favorable.
principles.application: Group 2 ($ns^2$) and Group 15 ($ns^2 np^3$) have higher IE than expected; Group 18 ($ns^2 np^6$) has positive EA.
principles.analogy: Like a completed puzzle: a finished puzzle (full subshell) is stable and resists having pieces removed (high IE) or added (positive EA).
principles.example: N ($2p^3$, half-filled) has higher IE ($1402$ kJ/mol) than O ($2p^4$, $1314$ kJ/mol) despite lower $Z$, due to half-filled subshell stability.

image.alt: Orbital diagrams showing stable electron configurations
image.caption: Stable electron configurations (full/half-full subshells) affect IE and EA.

principles.statement: Paired electrons in the same orbital repel each other, making removal easier (lower IE) and addition less favorable (less negative EA).
principles.application: Explains why Group 16 has lower IE than Group 15, and why F has less negative EA than Cl despite higher $Z$.
principles.analogy: Like crowded seating: two people (electrons) in one seat (orbital) push against each other, making it easier for one to leave (lower IE).
principles.example: O ($2p^4$, one paired orbital) has lower IE than N ($2p^3$, all unpaired); F has less negative EA than Cl due to repulsion in small 2p orbital.

image.alt: Diagram showing electron-electron repulsion in paired orbitals
image.caption: Electron-electron repulsion in paired orbitals affects IE and EA values.

sections.section_title: 4.9.8 Applications of IE and EA Trends
sections.introduction: Understanding ionization energy and electron affinity trends enables prediction of chemical behavior, bonding patterns, and material properties.

process_types.definition: Large difference in IE and EA between elements predicts ionic bond formation
process_types.role: Identify which element pairs will form ionic vs. covalent compounds
process_types.function: Metals (low IE) transfer electrons to nonmetals (very negative EA)
goals: Design ionic materials with specific properties
Explain solubility patterns of ionic compounds

primary_example.explanation: Na has low IE ($496$ kJ/mol), easily loses electron; Cl has very negative EA ($-349$ kJ/mol), readily gains electron. Large energy difference drives ionic bond formation: $Na + Cl \rightarrow Na^+Cl^-$.

image.alt: Sodium transferring electron to chlorine
image.caption: Large IE/EA difference between metal and nonmetal drives ionic bonding.

examples.explanation: Mg (IE₁ + IE₂ = $2188$ kJ/mol) transfers 2 electrons to O (EA = $-141$ kJ/mol), forming Mg²⁺O²⁻ ionic compound.
K (IE = $419$ kJ/mol) transfers electron to Br (EA = $-325$ kJ/mol), forming K⁺Br⁻ ionic compound.

process_types.definition: Similar IE and EA values between elements predict covalent bond formation through electron sharing
process_types.role: Identify which element pairs will share electrons rather than transfer them
process_types.function: Nonmetals with similar IE and EA share electrons to achieve stable configurations
goals: Understand bond polarity and dipole moments
Design organic molecules with specific properties
Explain reactivity patterns in organic chemistry

primary_example.explanation: H (IE = $1312$ kJ/mol) and O (IE = $1314$ kJ/mol) have similar, high IE values. Neither easily loses electrons, so they share electrons in covalent bonds: $2H + O \rightarrow H_2O$.

image.alt: Hydrogen and oxygen sharing electrons in water molecule
image.caption: Similar IE/EA values between nonmetals favor covalent bonding.

examples.explanation: C and O both have high IE and negative EA; they share electrons in double bonds to form linear CO₂ molecule.
C and H share electrons in four single bonds, forming tetrahedral methane molecule.

process_types.definition: IE and EA values predict which elements will be most reactive as metals or nonmetals
process_types.role: Predict chemical reactivity and select appropriate elements for specific applications
process_types.function: Lowest IE = most reactive metals; most negative EA = most reactive nonmetals
goals: Select oxidizing agents (very negative EA nonmetals)

primary_example.explanation: Cs has lowest IE ($376$ kJ/mol) of stable elements, making it the most reactive metal. It reacts explosively with water, air, and halogens. Used in specialized applications requiring extreme reactivity.

image.alt: Cesium reacting violently with water
image.caption: Lowest ionization energy correlates with highest metal reactivity.

examples.explanation: F has most negative EA ($-328$ kJ/mol), making it the strongest oxidizing agent. It reacts with nearly all elements, even noble gases under extreme conditions.
Li-ion batteries use Li (low IE) as anode material; electrons flow from low IE anode to high EA cathode, generating electrical current.

sections.relationship: IE and EA trends connect to other periodic properties: low IE correlates with large atomic radius and low electronegativity (metals); very negative EA correlates with small radius and high electronegativity (nonmetals).

summary: Ionization energy (IE) is the energy required to remove an electron from a gaseous atom ($M(g) \rightarrow M^+(g) + e^-$); electron affinity (EA) is the energy change when an electron is added ($X(g) + e^- \rightarrow X^-(g)$). IE increases across periods (increasing $Z_{eff}$) and decreases down groups (increasing radius and shielding), with exceptions at Groups 2-13 and 15-16 due to electron configuration effects. EA generally becomes more negative across periods (halogens most negative) and less negative down groups, but trends are less regular than IE. Successive ionization energies increase progressively, with large jumps when removing core electrons after valence electrons are removed, revealing the number of valence electrons. Factors affecting both properties include atomic radius (larger = lower IE, less negative EA), nuclear charge (higher = higher IE, more negative EA), electron configuration (stable configurations increase IE), and electron-electron repulsion (paired electrons lower IE, reduce EA negativity). Applications include predicting ionic vs. covalent bonding (large IE/EA difference = ionic; similar values = covalent), understanding reactivity patterns (lowest IE = most reactive metals; most negative EA = most reactive nonmetals), and designing electrochemical devices like batteries. These energy trends connect to electronegativity, atomic radius, and chemical bonding behavior, forming a foundation for understanding periodic chemical properties.
